nodes.title: Normalize each input with Promise.resolve
nodes.why: This lets the function handle both plain values and promises in one path, just like native Promise.all.
nodes.alternative: Assume every item is already a promise and call `.then` directly.
nodes.tradeoff: Slightly more overhead, but much more reliable behavior for mixed inputs.

nodes.title: Write results by original index
nodes.why: Promises can resolve in any order, but users expect output to match input order.
nodes.tradeoff: Index writes preserve contract; pushing is simpler but gives wrong order.

nodes.title: Reject immediately when one input fails
nodes.why: Promise.all is fail-fast. The first rejection should end the outer promise right away.
nodes.alternative: Collect both successes and failures and resolve with a combined report.
nodes.tradeoff: Fail-fast matches standard Promise.all, but you lose partial success details.

nodes.title: Validate input before processing
nodes.why: A clear early check gives predictable errors and avoids confusing runtime behavior later.
nodes.alternative: Let the loop fail naturally on invalid input.
nodes.tradeoff: Extra guard code, but much better error clarity.

nodes.title: Await each item one by one
nodes.why: This is easy to read and reason about because control flow is fully linear.
nodes.alternative: Start all promises in parallel and track completion with a counter.
nodes.tradeoff: Very readable, but slower when many async tasks could run in parallel.

nodes.title: Keep output order aligned with input
nodes.why: Using index assignment ensures returned values stay aligned with the original positions.
nodes.alternative: Build a new list using helper methods like `.map()`.
nodes.tradeoff: Index assignment is explicit and clear for async loops, though less compact.
